topic: Fundamental Duties & Directive Principles of State Policy (DPSP)

questions.text: The Directive Principles of State Policy in the Indian Constitution have been borrowed from the Constitution of which country?
questions.explanation: The DPSP (Articles 36–51) were borrowed from the Irish Constitution of 1937. Ireland itself drew this concept from the idea of a just social order. The framers liked the idea of listing aspirational goals for the State that are not judicially enforceable.
questions.source: SSC CGL — one of the most standard 'borrowed from which constitution' Polity questions

questions.text: Which Constitutional Amendment added Fundamental Duties to the Indian Constitution?
questions.explanation: The 42nd Amendment (1976), passed during the Emergency under Indira Gandhi's government, added Part IVA and Article 51A with 10 Fundamental Duties. A 11th duty was later added by the 86th Amendment (2002).
questions.source: SSC CGL — 42nd Amendment and Fundamental Duties is a recurring pairing in Polity PYQs

questions.text: The Directive Principles of State Policy are contained in which Part of the Indian Constitution?
questions.explanation: Directive Principles are in Part IV, Articles 36–51. Fundamental Rights are in Part III (Articles 12–35). Fundamental Duties were added as Part IVA (Article 51A) by the 42nd Amendment, 1976.
questions.source: SSC CGL — Part/Article mapping for DPSPs is a standard question pattern

questions.text: Which committee recommended the inclusion of Fundamental Duties in the Indian Constitution?
questions.explanation: The Swaran Singh Committee (1976) recommended the inclusion of Fundamental Duties, drawing inspiration from the Soviet Constitution. The committee suggested 8 duties; the government ultimately codified 10 (with a 11th added in 2002).
questions.source: SSC CGL — Swaran Singh Committee and Fundamental Duties is a well-known PYQ pairing

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a Directive Principle of State Policy?
options: Protection of monuments of national importance (Article 49)
questions.explanation: Abolition of untouchability is a Fundamental Right under Article 17 (Part III), not a DPSP. All the others — Uniform Civil Code (Art 44), Separation of judiciary (Art 50), and Protection of monuments (Art 49) — are Directive Principles in Part IV.
questions.source: SSC CGL — 'which is NOT a DPSP' pattern appears regularly, often mixing FRs with DPSPs as distractors

questions.text: The Supreme Court in Minerva Mills v. Union of India (1980) primarily held that:
options: Directive Principles are superior to Fundamental Rights
Fundamental Rights are superior to Directive Principles
Harmony between Fundamental Rights and Directive Principles is itself part of the Basic Structure
Parliament can amend Fundamental Rights to give effect to any Directive Principle
questions.explanation: Minerva Mills (1980) struck down the expanded version of Article 31C (42nd Amendment) that had tried to make DPSPs override FRs 14 and 19. The SC held that the balance and harmony between FRs and DPSPs is a feature of the Basic Structure — one cannot be completely subordinated to the other. Neither is 'superior'; both must coexist.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated based on the Minerva Mills significance question pattern in higher-level SSC papers

questions.text: Article 45 of the Constitution, which originally dealt with free and compulsory education for children, was modified by the 86th Amendment (2002). What does Article 45 cover now?
options: Early childhood care and education for children below the age of 6
Free secondary education for children aged 14–18
questions.explanation: After the 86th Amendment (2002) moved the 6–14 age group into Article 21A as a Fundamental Right, Article 45 was rephrased to cover early childhood care and education for children below age 6. The two articles now work together: 21A (6–14, enforceable FR) and 45 (0–6, aspirational DPSP).
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — a classic trap because students remember old Article 45 without knowing the 86th Amendment rewrote it

questions.text: Fundamental Duties under Article 51A apply to:
options: All persons residing in India including foreign nationals
questions.explanation: Fundamental Duties (Article 51A) apply exclusively to *citizens* of India — not to foreign nationals or aliens residing in India. DPSPs, by contrast, are directed at the State. FRs are available to 'persons' (which includes foreigners for most rights), but FDs are citizen-specific.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated based on the common confusion between who FDs and DPSPs bind

questions.text: Article 39A, which requires the State to ensure equal justice and free legal aid, is classified as which type of DPSP?
options: It is a Fundamental Right, not a DPSP
questions.explanation: Article 39A falls under the Socialist DPSPs — it requires the State to secure that the operation of the legal system promotes justice on the basis of equal opportunity, and in particular to provide free legal aid to ensure justice isn't denied because of economic inability. It sits alongside Articles 38–43 as part of the welfare-state cluster.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because students know Article 39 well but overlook 39A's separate classification

questions.text: The 11th Fundamental Duty, added by the 86th Amendment (2002), places an obligation on:
options: The State to provide free schooling
Children to attend school between ages 6 and 14
Parents or guardians to provide education opportunities for their child aged 6–14
questions.explanation: The 11th Fundamental Duty [Article 51A(k)] says: 'It shall be the duty of every citizen who is a parent or guardian to provide opportunities for education to his child or ward between the age of six and fourteen years.' The child gets the right (Article 21A); the parent gets the corresponding duty. This pairing is rarely tested but logically elegant.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because most students know only 10 duties and miss the 2002 addition

questions.text: Which DPSP forms the constitutional basis for states that have enacted laws prohibiting the consumption of alcohol (like Gujarat)?
questions.explanation: Article 47 explicitly directs the State to bring about prohibition of intoxicating drinks and drugs that are injurious to health. When states like Gujarat, Bihar, or Manipur enact prohibition laws and these are challenged, the courts have upheld them partly on the basis of Article 47.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because students memorize Article 47 as 'nutrition' but forget it also covers prohibition

questions.text: Which of the following statements about Directive Principles of State Policy is CORRECT?
options: They are justiciable — courts can strike down laws that violate DPSPs
They are non-justiciable but are fundamental in the governance of the country
They were added by the 42nd Amendment during the Emergency
They apply to citizens, not to the State
questions.explanation: Article 37 explicitly states that DPSPs 'shall not be enforceable by any court' (non-justiciable) but are 'fundamental in the governance of the country.' They apply to the State (not citizens), were part of the original 1950 Constitution (not the 42nd Amendment), and courts cannot strike down laws for merely violating DPSPs.
questions.source: One of the most frequently tested 'true/false about DPSPs' patterns across SSC papers

questions.text: The Uniform Civil Code, one of the most debated constitutional provisions, is mentioned in which Article and Part of the Constitution?
questions.explanation: The Uniform Civil Code is mentioned as a Directive Principle under Article 44, Part IV. It directs the State to endeavour to secure a Uniform Civil Code for all citizens, but it is aspirational and non-justiciable — it has NOT been enacted as law. This distinction (DPSP, not FR or enacted law) is frequently tested.
questions.source: Article 44 and UCC is a standard recurring question in SSC Polity; the Part IV vs Part III distinction catches many students

questions.text: How many Fundamental Duties are currently listed in the Indian Constitution?
questions.explanation: There are currently 11 Fundamental Duties. The original 10 were added by the 42nd Amendment (1976) based on the Swaran Singh Committee recommendations. The 11th — duty of parents/guardians to provide education opportunities to their child between 6 and 14 — was added by the 86th Amendment (2002).
questions.source: The 10 vs 11 distinction is a consistent trap in SSC papers — many students learn '10 duties' and miss the 2002 addition
